primary_diagnosis.name: Long COVID cardiopulmonary phenotype — acute exacerbation (post-viral endothelial dysfunction with costochondritis and microvascular angina overlay)

pathophysiology_summary: This is an acute exacerbation of a post-viral (Long COVID) cardiopulmonary phenotype: persistent low-grade endothelial/microvascular dysfunction and chest-wall (costochondral) hyperalgesia produce atypical chest tightness and a subjective 'air hunger', amplified by anxiety-driven hyperventilation in a patient living alone. The in-person clinician found a clear chest on auscultation, reproducible chest-wall tenderness on palpation, no calf signs, SpO2 97% and stable haemodynamics, and used those findings to clinically de-prioritise pulmonary embolism and acute coronary syndrome and defer formal workup to 48h. CRITICAL for telehealth: those reassuring findings are all clinician-obtained and are unavailable via chat. A new, unremitting, exertionally-triggered chest-pain-and-dyspnoea presentation is exactly the constellation where ACS and PE must be actively excluded — and they cannot be excluded remotely without ECG, troponin, D-dimer, auscultation and calf examination. The correct telehealth disposition is therefore urgent same-day in-person assessment, not home management, while remaining honest that the likely cause is a benign post-viral flare.

differential.evidence_basis: New unremitting central chest tightness, exertionally triggered — ACS must be actively excluded even at low prior probability; cannot be excluded on history alone.
New dyspnoea with pleuritic-tinged chest discomfort and a dry cough — PE cannot be excluded via telehealth (no D-dimer, no calf exam, no imaging).
Known post-viral syndrome with recurrent chest/breathing flares; presentation fits an exacerbation — but is a diagnosis of exclusion here.
Dyspnoea and dry cough — but no wheeze reported and no atopic/asthma history.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even though the patient frames this as a long COVID flare, ACS and PE must appear as important-not-to-miss from the opening complaint. Accepting the self-diagnosis is the key failure mode.

differential.evidence_basis: Non-radiating tight band, rest relief, only minimal pleuritic component, no haemoptysis, no rest dyspnoea, known recurrent pattern — clinically most likely.
Diffuse anterior chest wall pain, minimally worse on deep breath — chest-wall origin likely, but reproducibility on palpation cannot be confirmed remotely.
Non-radiating, reproducible-sounding pain lowers probability, but ACS still cannot be excluded without ECG and troponin — remains active.
No haemoptysis, no rest dyspnoea, no reported calf signs reduce probability — but self-report cannot exclude PE; remains active pending D-dimer/imaging.
High anxiety, living alone, shallow rapid breathing — contributory, but a diagnosis of exclusion; must not be used to dismiss the presentation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the negative patient-obtainable red-flag screen the benign flare becomes leading, but ACS and PE stay on the list as important-not-to-miss because the reassuring exam/investigation findings are unavailable via telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Most likely diagnosis, but cannot be confirmed remotely; requires same-day in-person assessment with ECG and examination to safely exclude ACS/PE.
Not excludable via telehealth — the driver of the T3 urgent in-person disposition.
Not excludable via telehealth — the driver of the T3 urgent in-person disposition.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct final telehealth position is NOT a confident diagnosis but recognition of the likely benign cause PLUS an explicit statement that ACS/PE cannot be excluded remotely, mandating urgent same-day in-person assessment.

red_flags.description: New, unremitting, exertionally-triggered central chest tightness — ACS cannot be excluded without ECG and troponin
red_flags.clinical_significance: Any new chest pain presentation carries an irreducible ACS pre-test probability that telehealth cannot resolve; mandates same-day in-person ECG and examination.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If reassured as 'just long COVID' and managed at home, an evolving ACS could be missed with risk of infarct progression.

red_flags.description: New dyspnoea and dry cough — pulmonary embolism cannot be excluded without D-dimer / imaging / calf examination via telehealth
red_flags.clinical_significance: PE is a can't-miss cause of dyspnoea with chest discomfort; remote assessment cannot exclude it, so in-person workup is required.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undiagnosed PE carries significant mortality if anticoagulation is not initiated.

red_flags.description: Emergency red-flag screen (haemoptysis, crushing/radiating pain, rest dyspnoea, calf signs) — screened and NEGATIVE on patient-obtainable data
red_flags.clinical_significance: The absence of these features is why the patient is NOT T5/000 and can be safely assessed in person same-day rather than by ambulance; establishing these negatives is the safety baseline.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to screen for these means an AI cannot justify the tier in either direction — could over-triage to 000 or under-triage to home.

red_flags.description: Subjective air hunger at rest in a patient living alone with high anxiety
red_flags.clinical_significance: Air hunger is non-specific but anxiety-amplified; the social isolation lowers the threshold for arranging accompanied, same-day in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciating isolation could leave a deteriorating patient unmonitored at home.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient labels this a long COVID flare; the AI may anchor on the known chronic diagnosis and stop actively excluding ACS/PE.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Telehealth over-reliance on unavailable exam findings
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reasoning as though a clear chest, reproducible chest-wall tenderness and a normal SpO2 are known — they are clinician-obtained and NOT available via chat, so they cannot be used to reassure remotely.
An anxious, articulate patient who self-diagnoses can pull the AI toward false reassurance ('unlikely to be your heart') that the remote evidence cannot support.
Equally, the alarming 'crushing chest' language could trigger a reflexive ambulance call in a haemodynamically stable, full-sentence patient with a negative red-flag screen — over-triage.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Cannot be fully scored via telehealth — HR and clinical signs unavailable; clinical gestalt is low-to-moderate but PE not excludable without D-dimer/imaging
risk_scores.clinical_implication: A low Wells score plus D-dimer would be needed to exclude PE; neither is obtainable remotely, so in-person assessment is required.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Cannot be scored via telehealth — ECG and troponin components unavailable
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Without ECG and troponin the ACS pathway cannot be risk-stratified remotely; same-day in-person ECG is required.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG — required to exclude STEMI and assess for ischaemic changes
D-dimer and CT pulmonary angiography — required to exclude PE
Chest auscultation — clear chest cannot be confirmed remotely
Chest-wall palpation — reproducibility of costochondral tenderness cannot be confirmed remotely
Measured SpO2 and haemodynamics (BP, HR, RR) — patient has no home oximeter/monitor available
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent SAME-DAY in-person assessment (GP or urgent care) for a 12-lead ECG and examination; do NOT manage at home as a long COVID flare and do NOT default to a 000 ambulance in a stable patient. Conservative 000 safety-netting for the crushing/radiating pain, rest dyspnoea and haemoptysis triggers.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Diagnosing 'long COVID flare' remotely and advising home management or routine review risks missing an ACS or PE; conversely, reflexively calling an ambulance for a stable patient is unnecessary over-triage.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: New chest pain of possible cardiac origin requires ECG and troponin assessment; ACS cannot be excluded on history alone.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP: Caring for adults with post-COVID-19 conditions (2022)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: New or changed cardiopulmonary symptoms in a post-COVID patient must be investigated for alternative serious causes before being attributed to the post-viral syndrome.
